sourceTitle: Notify people instantly — with just a click of a button

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using a button in Notion as described in the guide?

options.text: Automate repetitive tasks such as sending notifications
options.explanation: Buttons let you trigger actions like notifications, emails, or webhooks, turning a manual step into a single click.

options.text: Change the page theme automatically
options.explanation: Changing a page theme is not a function of buttons; they trigger actions, not styling.

options.text: Export page data to a CSV file
options.explanation: Exporting data is not mentioned as a button capability in the article.

options.text: Create a new workspace
options.explanation: Buttons operate within a page or database; they cannot create entire workspaces.

questions.question: How many specific people can you select for an in‑app notification action using a button?

options.explanation: The limit is higher than 5.
The guide states a limit of 20, not 10.
You can select up to 20 people for an in‑app notification.

options.text: Unlimited
options.explanation: There is a defined limit of 20, so unlimited is incorrect.

questions.question: Which command do you type to insert a new button into a Notion page?

options.text: Ctrl+B
options.explanation: Notion uses slash commands, not keyboard shortcuts, for inserting blocks.

options.text: /button
options.explanation: Typing /button brings up the button block.

options.text: Alt+N
options.explanation: Alt+N is not a Notion shortcut for buttons.

options.text: Shift+Enter
options.explanation: Shift+Enter creates a line break, not a button.

questions.question: When configuring a button for critical‑issue notifications, which action should be placed first in the action list?

options.text: Show confirmation popup
options.explanation: Placing the confirmation first ensures users approve the action before any notifications are sent.

options.text: Send in‑app notification
options.explanation: If this runs before confirmation, the notification could be sent unintentionally.

options.text: Send email to team leads
options.explanation: Email should also follow confirmation to avoid accidental alerts.

options.text: Add a post‑click confirmation note
options.explanation: The post‑click note appears after actions; it does not prevent accidental clicks.

questions.question: Which of the following cannot be sent directly by a Notion button action according to the guide?

options.text: In‑app notification
options.explanation: In‑app notifications are a supported button action.

options.text: Email via Gmail
options.explanation: The guide shows sending email as a button action.

options.text: SMS via Twilio
options.explanation: SMS is not mentioned as a native button action; it would require an external integration not covered here.

options.text: Webhook to Zendesk
options.explanation: Webhooks are explicitly listed as a way to trigger external actions.

questions.question: How can you ensure a button notifies the correct on‑call person using data from another database?

options.text: Hard‑code the person's email address in the button
options.explanation: Hard‑coding would not adapt to schedule changes.

options.text: Use a custom formula that references the on‑call database
options.explanation: Formulas can pull values from related databases, allowing dynamic targeting.

options.text: Manually select the person each time the button is clicked
options.explanation: Manual selection defeats the purpose of automation.

options.text: Add a static text block with the person's name
options.explanation: Static text cannot drive the notification recipient.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the "Show confirmation" option in a button's action list?

options.text: To display a success message after the button is clicked
options.explanation: That describes a post‑click note, not the confirmation popup.

options.text: To prevent accidental clicks by asking the user to confirm the action
options.explanation: The confirmation popup asks the user to verify before any actions run.

options.text: To automatically email the user who clicked the button
options.explanation: The option does not trigger email; it only shows a prompt.

options.text: To change the button's color after it is pressed
options.explanation: Button styling is not controlled by the confirmation option.

questions.question: Which description best matches a multi‑step content review workflow created with a button?

options.text: One click sends a single email to all reviewers at once
options.explanation: The guide emphasizes sequential notifications, not a single bulk email.

options.text: The button notifies each reviewer in order, updating the status after each step
options.explanation: Multi‑step workflows can chain actions so each team is alerted in sequence.

options.text: The button creates a PDF of the content for distribution
options.explanation: PDF creation is not mentioned as a button capability in this guide.

options.text: The button archives the content automatically after review
options.explanation: Archiving is not part of the described multi‑step review process.

questions.question: If a button’s action list places "Send email" before "Show confirmation", what is the most likely result when the button is clicked?

options.text: Both the email and any other actions run without showing a confirmation popup
options.explanation: Actions execute in order; placing confirmation later means it never appears before the email is sent.

options.text: The email will only be sent after the user confirms
options.explanation: The confirmation appears after the email, so it cannot gate the email sending.

options.text: The confirmation popup never appears at all
options.explanation: The popup will still appear, but only after the email has already been sent.

options.text: The button will produce an error and stop all actions
options.explanation: The order does not cause an error; it just bypasses the intended safety check.

questions.question: Regarding formulas used in button actions, which statement is true?

options.text: Formulas can only reference properties on the same page
options.explanation: Formulas can reference related databases, not just the same page.

options.text: Formulas can pull data from other databases to personalize notifications
options.explanation: The guide describes using formulas to connect information from other databases for smarter notifications.

options.text: Formulas are not supported in email actions
options.explanation: Email actions can use formulas to customize subject lines and body content.

options.text: Formulas must be written in JavaScript
options.explanation: Notion formulas use Notion's own formula language, not JavaScript.
